name: Salesforce, Inc.

profile.description: Salesforce, Inc. provides customer relationship management (CRM) technology and enterprise cloud software. Core offerings include Sales Cloud, Service Cloud, Marketing Cloud, Commerce Cloud, and the Agentforce autonomous AI agent platform. Salesforce is the global #1 CRM vendor by revenue, serving enterprises across every major industry. The company completed the $8B acquisition of Informatica in November 2025 to strengthen its data management and AI foundation. FY2026 revenue of $41.5 billion; headquartered in San Francisco, California.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): Salesforce executed a fundamental business model transformation with Agentforce — pivoting from per-seat subscription pricing to per-task agentic AI pricing, a structural shift in how enterprise software is monetized [1][4]. The $8B Informatica acquisition (closed November 2025) adds data management infrastructure essential for AI agent accuracy [3]. These represent significant pivots opening new revenue streams, but the transformation is still in early innings — not yet the multi-decade validation that would earn 3 points. AI Deployment (2/2): Agentforce has generated $800M ARR in its first year, growing 169% YoY, with 29,000 cumulative deals and 12,000 enterprise customers adopting the platform [1][4]. This is concrete, quantified AI revenue — a textbook 2-point score. Market Share (2/2): Salesforce holds the #1 CRM position globally by revenue — a category-defining position maintained through multiple technology cycles [3]. Agentforce is establishing early category leadership in enterprise agentic AI, with management citing 50% quarter-over-quarter deal growth [1]. Product Pipeline (1/2): Agentforce 360 with 85% AI resolution rates is announced, and the Informatica data integration roadmap provides visibility into future capability, but specific launch dates and revenue guidance for next-generation products are not yet granular [4]. R&D Intensity (1/1): R&D spending of $5.99B = 14.4% of revenue — well above the software industry average of ~10-12%, reflecting serious long-term capability investment [2].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): Salesforce's purpose — 'We bring companies and customers together' — combined with the Ohana (family) culture and the Pledge 1% model creates a genuinely distinctive corporate identity [5]. Marc Benioff founded the Pledge 1% movement, committing 1% of equity, product, and time to philanthropy — a model now adopted by 10,000+ companies globally. The mission is specific, thoughtful, and connects the company's commercial work to community impact. However, the 2023 mass layoffs of 8,000 employees (10% of workforce) under activist shareholder pressure undermined the Ohana narrative, suggesting the culture is not yet the 50-year validated competitive advantage that earns 4 points [3]. Community Impact (2/2): Salesforce.org deploys CRM technology to 50,000+ nonprofits and educational institutions; the Pledge 1% model has generated over $1.4B in charitable value; Trailhead provides free online learning to 16M+ people globally [5]. These are specific, quantified community programs with meaningful scale — a clear 2-point score. Employee Retention (1/2): The 2023 layoffs of 8,000 employees followed by further restructuring in 2024-2025 under activist pressure create a difficult backdrop for retention claims [3]. The Ohana culture remains aspirationally strong but the organizational instability of the past three years prevents a 2-point score. Insider Buying (0/2): Marc Benioff has primarily been a net seller of CRM shares over the past 12 months; no meaningful net insider buying identified from key executives. The $800M Agentforce milestone was celebrated externally but not backed by insider purchasing conviction [6].

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics — among the strongest infrastructure profiles in large-cap enterprise software. D/E of 29.9% scores 1.0 (well below 70% target). ROE of 12.4% scores 0.827 (below 15% target; 2× weight). Revenue growth YoY of 12.1% scores 1.0 (above 10% target). 3-year revenue CAGR of 9.8% scores 1.0 (approaching 10% target; 2× weight). Forward P/E of 11.7 scores 1.0 (well below 20 target — notably cheap for a high-quality software franchise). Profit margin of 18.0% scores 0.898 (near 20% target). Forward PEG of 0.963 scores 1.0 (below 1.0 target; 2× weight) [6]. Liquidity passes: 13.2M daily volume × $177.51 = $2.34B daily. Weighted sum: (1.0×1) + (0.827×2) + (1.0×1) + (1.0×2) + (1.0×1) + (0.898×1) + (1.0×2) = 9.55 / 10 × 10 = 9.55 → 10. Free cash flow of $16.4B and $9.6B cash with only $17.7B total debt represent a highly liquid and well-capitalized business [6].

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): Salesforce occupies an unusual position — it IS building enterprise AI agents (Agentforce) while also having a legacy per-seat business that AI agents could theoretically cannibalize. Gartner projects 35% of point-product SaaS tools will be replaced by AI agents by 2030, and Bain/Deloitte published reports in late 2025 warning that AI agents could cannibalize per-seat revenue within 24 months [1][3]. However, Salesforce's response — transitioning to per-task Agentforce pricing and acquiring Informatica for data management — demonstrates active mitigation. The CRM data moat (decades of proprietary customer interaction data) is uniquely valuable as AI agent training material. Salesforce is both protected by data moats and actively reinventing to lead the disruption rather than be disrupted. Score of 7 reflects genuine transition risk alongside structural moat protection. AI Opportunity (1/1): Agentforce generated $800M ARR in its first full year, growing 169% YoY with 29,000 cumulative deals — concrete, quantified AI revenue that clearly earns the tiebreaker point [1][4].

infinite_game.summary: Salesforce under Marc Benioff exhibits genuine infinite-game characteristics — Pledge 1% as an industry-transforming Just Cause, courageous bets on Agentforce that cannibalize legacy revenue, and the Informatica acquisition doubling down on long-term data infrastructure. The transition from seat-based to task-based pricing is exactly the kind of existential flexibility Sinek celebrates. However, the 2023 activist-driven layoffs of 8,000 employees, Benioff's pattern of insider selling, and periodic strategic zigzags (buying and then questioning Twitter) reveal finite-game pressures. Salesforce plays the infinite game but is not immune to finite-game impulses.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): Salesforce's cause — creating a business model that serves customers, employees, communities, and shareholders equally (the stakeholder capitalism model) — is distinctive and has been partially validated by the Pledge 1% movement that Benioff founded and that has now spread to 10,000+ companies [5]. The Ohana culture, Salesforce.org's work with nonprofits, and Trailhead's free education platform are genuine expressions of this cause. However, the 2023 mass layoffs under Elliott Management pressure and Benioff's public ambivalence about priorities during activist battles suggest the cause has not been consistently defended at cost to financial performance — a Sinek requirement for the full 3 points [3].

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): The 2023 layoffs of 8,000 employees (approximately 10% of the workforce) following Elliott Management's activist campaign created a significant trust rupture in what had been an industry-celebrated Ohana culture [3]. Salesforce had historically ranked among the top employers with exceptional retention, but the layoff cycle and subsequent restructuring created organizational instability. The company's culture remains aspirationally strong — the Ohana framework, Trailhead investment, and Dreamforce community events reflect genuine investment in teams. However, Sinek's framework demands evidence that leadership protects people even at financial cost, and the 2023 decision tested that standard and came up short.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): Salesforce's competitive language has often leaned finite — Benioff has a well-documented history of targeting Microsoft, Oracle, and SAP explicitly, framing competition as market-share battles and vendor displacement. The company regularly publishes comparative benchmarks and 'why Salesforce vs. Microsoft' materials that reflect a winning-focused competitive mindset rather than a learn-from-rivals approach. Despite Agentforce being a legitimate innovation, the competitive framing around Agentforce has centered on displacing Microsoft Copilot and 'winning' the enterprise AI race — quintessentially finite game language.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (2/2): The shift from per-seat SaaS pricing to per-task agentic AI pricing with Agentforce is exactly the kind of existential flexibility Sinek champions — Salesforce is voluntarily disrupting its own most profitable revenue model (per-seat subscriptions generating ~$35B+ annually) in service of the longer-term vision [1][4]. The $8B Informatica acquisition represents a fundamental infrastructure bet, not a bolt-on. The willingness to trade short-term seat revenue certainty for long-term platform relevance — while existing customers could theoretically accomplish more with fewer seats — reflects genuinely courageous strategic reinvention.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (2/2): Salesforce has made multiple costly long-term bets in recent years: the $8B Informatica acquisition to build the data infrastructure for AI agents, the launch of Agentforce knowing it could cannibalize per-seat revenue, and sustained R&D investment of $6B annually (14.4% of revenue) even as activist shareholders pushed for margin expansion [3][4]. Robin Washington's appointment as COFO to lead the Agentforce transformation represents leadership continuity around a bold strategic bet rather than retreating to margin protection. Multiple examples of choosing long-term capability over short-term earnings qualify as a pattern of courage.

investment_thesis: Salesforce is executing the most consequential business model transformation in enterprise software: transitioning from per-seat SaaS subscriptions to per-task agentic AI pricing via Agentforce. With $800M in Agentforce ARR growing 169% YoY, 29,000 cumulative deals, and 12,000 enterprise customers already adopting, the platform's early traction validates the strategic bet [1][4]. At 11.7x forward earnings with a PEG of 0.963, Salesforce trades at an unusual discount for a #1-market-position software franchise — the market appears to be pricing AI disruption risk into the stock without fully crediting the Agentforce revenue ramp. The $16.4B annual free cash flow and $8B Informatica acquisition provide the data infrastructure moat that makes Salesforce's AI agents more accurate than competitors without proprietary CRM data.
thesis_continued: The primary risk is the per-seat to per-task transition itself. Bain/Deloitte analyst reports from late 2025 warned that AI agents could cannibalize per-seat revenue within 24 months, and Gartner projects 35% of point-product SaaS tools displaced by AI agents by 2030 [1]. If Agentforce achieves its efficiency promises, customers may accomplish the same outcomes with fewer Salesforce seats, and the per-task revenue may not fully offset seat-count contraction — a transition risk with no historical precedent to calibrate against. Additionally, Salesforce's Inspire Impact score of -67 (MISALIGNED) reflects social activism that creates reputational risks with values-aligned investors. The 2023 activist-driven layoffs and Benioff's net insider selling suggest limited personal financial conviction in the current stock price [3][6].
key_catalysts: Agentforce ARR reaching $2B+ by end of FY2027, demonstrating that per-task pricing more than offsets any per-seat compression and validating the full business model transformation

Informatica data management integration driving new enterprise deals where CRM + data + AI agents are sold as an integrated platform, creating switching costs that defend against Microsoft Copilot displacement
Agentforce 360 with 85% AI resolution rates achieving broad enterprise adoption, with management providing specific productivity and ROI metrics that compel accelerated deployment
key_risks: Per-seat to per-task revenue cannibalization: if Agentforce enables customers to accomplish more with fewer seats, total revenue growth could slow faster than Agentforce ARR scales — the transition math is uncertain and unprecedented

Microsoft Copilot competitive pressure: Microsoft's deep Office365/Teams integration gives Copilot a distribution advantage that Agentforce cannot easily replicate, and Microsoft's $3T market cap means it can subsidize AI agent pricing to capture Salesforce enterprise accounts

Activist recurrence risk: Salesforce's stock is down sharply from its 52-week high of $276.80 to $177.51 — a decline of 36% — increasing the probability of renewed activist pressure that could force further restructuring and undermine the multi-year Agentforce investment cycle
